_metadata.purpose: Easter egg data pool — seeded from GeekTheViking Data-Coffer (Long Hall, Artifacts, Echoes)

echoes.quote: Cyberspace does not lie within your borders.
echoes.context: A Declaration of the Independence of Cyberspace, 1996

echoes.quote: Given enough eyeballs, all bugs are shallow.
echoes.context: Linus's Law — The Cathedral and the Bazaar, 1997

echoes.quote: We shape our tools, and thereafter our tools shape us.
Software is getting slower more rapidly than hardware becomes faster.
echoes.context: Wirth's Law — A Plea for Lean Software, 1995

echoes.quote: Free as in freedom, not free as in beer.
If privacy is outlawed, only outlaws will have privacy.
The net interprets censorship as damage and routes around it.

echoes.context: The Whole Earth Catalog, 1974 — quoted by Steve Jobs at Stanford, 2005

echoes.quote: The future is already here — it is just not evenly distributed.
In Cyberspace, the First Amendment is a local ordinance.
Increasingly, people seem to misinterpret complexity as sophistication.
Any sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic.

personalities.name: Grace Hopper
personalities.fact: Found the first actual computer bug in 1947. It was a moth. Taped into her logbook. Entry: 'First actual case of bug being found.'

personalities.name: Linus Torvalds
personalities.fact: Created Linux at 21, in a university dorm room in Helsinki. First release: 10,239 lines of code. Now: 30+ million.

personalities.name: Aaron Swartz
personalities.fact: Co-authored the RSS 1.0 specification at age 13.

personalities.name: Ada Lovelace
personalities.fact: Wrote the first algorithm intended for a machine that didn't exist yet — Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine, 1843.

personalities.name: Alan Turing
personalities.fact: Built the Bombe machine that cracked Nazi Enigma ciphers in WWII. Historians estimate it shortened the war by 2 years.

personalities.name: Brian Kernighan
personalities.fact: Wrote 'Hello, World!' in 1972. Every programmer since has started there. He probably didn't know.

personalities.name: Phil Zimmermann
personalities.fact: Released PGP on the internet in 1991. The US government opened a criminal investigation. Three years later, they dropped the case.

personalities.name: John Perry Barlow
personalities.fact: Was a Grateful Dead lyricist for 20+ years AND co-founded the Electronic Frontier Foundation. Not a contradiction.

personalities.name: John McCarthy
personalities.fact: Coined the term 'Artificial Intelligence' in 1955. Also invented LISP in 1958. LISP is still used today.

personalities.name: Kevin Mitnick
personalities.fact: The FBI's most wanted hacker in the 1990s. His most powerful tool was never a computer — it was a phone and a convincing voice.

personalities.name: Douglas Engelbart
personalities.fact: Invented the computer mouse in 1964. Also invented hypertext, video conferencing, and collaborative real-time editing — all in the same decade.

personalities.name: Vint Cerf
personalities.fact: Co-designed TCP/IP with Bob Kahn in 1974. The protocol was built to survive nuclear war. It's still running.

personalities.name: Tim Berners-Lee
personalities.fact: Invented the World Wide Web in 1989 and gave it away for free. No patent. No company. Just a gift to the world.

personalities.name: Richard Stallman
personalities.fact: Started GNU in 1983. His goal: a completely free operating system. He is still working on it.

personalities.name: Claude Shannon
personalities.fact: Invented information theory in 1948. Proved that any message can be transmitted with arbitrarily low error. Every byte you send owes him.

artifacts.name: ARPANET First Message, 1969
artifacts.fact: The first message ever sent over the internet was 'LO'. It was supposed to be 'LOGIN'. The system crashed after two characters.

artifacts.name: The First Bug, 1947
artifacts.fact: A moth found in a Harvard Mark II relay by Grace Hopper's team. Taped into the logbook. The origin of 'debugging'.

artifacts.name: Linux 0.01, 1991
artifacts.fact: Linus Torvalds posted: 'I'm doing a (free) operating system (just a hobby, won't be big and professional like gnu).' The kernel now runs 97% of the world's top servers.

artifacts.name: TCP/IP Protocol, 1974
artifacts.fact: Designed to survive nuclear war by routing around damage. It became the language of the entire internet.

artifacts.name: PGP — Pretty Good Privacy, 1991
artifacts.fact: Phil Zimmermann uploaded it for free. The US government called it munitions export. The case lasted 3 years. PGP won.

artifacts.name: SSH Protocol, 1995
artifacts.fact: Created in 3 months after Tatu Ylönen's university network was sniffed. 20,000 users in 50 countries within months of release.

artifacts.name: Raspberry Pi, 2012
artifacts.fact: $35. 5 watts. Credit-card sized. 50 million units sold. Green IT made tangible.

artifacts.name: GNU Manifesto, 1985
artifacts.fact: Stallman: 'I consider that the Golden Rule requires that if I like a program I must share it with other people who like it.' The document that started the open source world.

artifacts.name: Bitcoin Genesis Block, 2009
artifacts.fact: Block 0. January 3, 2009. Embedded in the coinbase: 'The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks.' A timestamp and a manifesto.
